# Shield Claude - synced from DEVIL ~/.claude
<!-- GENERATED by tools/export-claude-env.ps1. Edit the PC global CLAUDE.md, not this. -->
<!-- Local-LLM offload + Windows-shell rules stripped: the Shield has no ollama. -->

# Global Claude Code rules

## Output
- Lead with the answer. No preamble. No "Great question." No closing summary when a diff or table already says it.
- One sentence per update during long work. Don't narrate deliberation.
- Default to educational-level comments in code. Don't waste words, don't mince words.

## README files — always bedazzled
Every `README.md` I create or substantially edit gets the full GitHub-flavored visual treatment **by default** — never plain text. Lean hard into it; the only brakes are accuracy and what GitHub actually renders. Shorthand to request or re-apply on any doc: **"bedazzle"** (e.g. "bedazzle the readme") → invoke the `bedazzle` skill for the full checklist.

## Subagent dispatch
Default model: **haiku**. Step up to **sonnet** when the task warrants it (sustained code writing, multi-file refactor, architectural reasoning). Use **opus** only on explicit user request.

Use judgment, but lean against subagent spawns. They're justified when (a) the work is genuinely independent and won't share state with the main thread, and (b) the token savings from off-thread reads beat the dispatch overhead. Borderline cases default to inline.

## Councils
Any request to convene, run, or consult a "council" (any phrasing, any repo) → invoke the `council` skill, which implements council-v2-spec: Spec → blind generation → adversarial verification → arbitration, arbiter = Fable 5. Never improvise an ad-hoc council.

## Context hygiene
At ~60% context fill, run `/compact focus on <current-task>`. Don't wait for the 83.5% auto-trigger.

Keep CLAUDE.md lean. Push detail into skills that load on demand.

## Tool selection
Prefer dedicated tools: **Glob** over `find`, **Grep** over `grep`/`rg`, **Read** over `cat`. Use **Bash** only when no dedicated tool fits.

Always batch independent tool calls in one turn. Two sequential tool calls with no dependency between them = a bug. Go back and parallelize.

## Git: default branch is `master`, not `main`

This user's convention is **`master`** as the default branch on every repo, everywhere. New repos: `git init -b master`. Existing repos still on `main`: when the user asks for a rename, do `git branch -m main master`, `git push -u origin master`, `gh api -X PATCH repos/<owner>/<repo> -f default_branch=master`, `git push origin --delete main`, `git remote set-head origin -a`. Never silently rename without being asked, but if asked, follow that exact sequence. The "main branch (you will usually use this for PRs)" line in session context is auto-detected by Claude Code — it'll say `master` once renamed.

## Git: commit and push proactively — label well so anything is revertable

Standing rule (user, 2026-06-07): **commit and push freely** as work reaches coherent checkpoints. Do NOT wait to be asked, and do NOT defer to end-of-session. The safety mechanism is **labeling, not gatekeeping** — write clear, specific commit messages (what changed + why) and keep commits small and well-scoped, so any single commit can be cleanly reverted if it turns out wrong. Prefer many small labelled commits over few large ones.

Default branch is `master`; commit directly there (this user's convention). Push after committing — `do NOT ask "should I push?"`, just push. Hardware dies; origin is the source of truth.

Only exception (still surface first): a push that would **rewrite or clobber remote history** — force-push, rebase of already-pushed commits, history drops. Those get a heads-up before they go up. Ordinary forward commits just go.

End every session at 100% sync parity regardless.

## Safety under --dangerously-skip-permissions
Hooks may block or `ask` even when permissions are bypassed. If a hook returns "ask", stop and surface the command to the human verbatim — do not paraphrase or auto-retry.
